summary: I've been living in Kentucky for about sixteen or seventeen years, with a few breaks in between. I ended up here around 2006 or 2007 after moving around a lot as a young adult involved in social justice activism. Kentucky, especially Lexington, became my home where I got involved in the punk scene, DIY organizing, and political activism. Over time, I pursued professional degrees related to natural resources and urban design here, which shaped my current work. Kentucky has been central to my personal growth and activism journey.

I grew up partly in the Washington, D.C. area after my parents immigrated from Brazil. It was an important place in my early life before I started moving around as a young adult. D.C. was part of my formative years before I eventually settled in Kentucky. My experiences there helped shape my understanding of social justice and activism.

Along with Washington, D.C., I spent a significant part of my childhood growing up in Maryland. This area was part of my early years after my family immigrated from Brazil. Maryland, together with D.C., provided the backdrop for my early experiences before I began traveling and engaging in activism across different cities. It was foundational to my understanding of community and social issues.

Before moving to Kentucky, I was living in California around the Central Valley in 2006 or so. It was part of my nomadic lifestyle where I moved from city to city, backpacking and engaging in social justice work. California was the last place I lived before heading to Lexington to help with a community center project. That period was part of my broader journey through different activist communities across the country.

I had a friend from Cincinnati who was an old pen pal, and he moved to Lexington to start a community center and info shop. His move was actually the reason I came to Lexington in 2007, to help out with that project. Although the community center was shut down before I arrived, Cincinnati played a key role in my decision to settle in Kentucky. That connection brought me into the Lexington activist and punk scenes.

Lexington is where I landed in 2007 and where I got deeply involved in the punk scene and DIY organizing. I came initially to help with a community center project, but after it was shut down, I stayed and became part of the local cultural and political movements. Over the years, Lexington has been the place where I learned wilderness survival, plant identification, and began my formal education at the University of Kentucky. It’s been central to both my activism and professional development.

I ended up attending the University of Kentucky about a decade after moving to Lexington. There, I earned professional degrees in natural resources, urban design, and stream and watershed work. This education was a turning point that helped me transition from grassroots activism to a more formal career in environmental and urban ecology fields. UK played a key role in shaping my current professional focus on climate adaptation and food sovereignty.

I witnessed different waves of Black Lives Matter activism in Lexington, especially comparing the movement in 2014 to its resurgence in 2020. The local scene involved some of the same people playing different roles over time. Black Lives Matter was part of the broader political landscape I was involved in, alongside other radical and community organizing efforts. It highlighted shifts in local activism and the evolving nature of social justice movements here.

I was deeply involved with Food Not Bombs in multiple cities, including Lexington, around 2007 and 2008. This organization focused on recovering food that would otherwise be thrown away and sharing it freely with unhoused people and others on the street. I even have a Food Not Bombs tattoo on my neck, which shows how important this work was to me. It was part of the DIY punk scene’s approach to direct action and mutual aid.

Before I arrived in Lexington, there had been a Reclaim the Streets event, which was a spontaneous block party and protest with an anarchist orientation. That event went poorly, with some people getting arrested after confrontations with police. This caused a split in the local punk scene about commitment to direct action. Reclaim the Streets was emblematic of the radical, ultra-left political culture I encountered when I moved here.

Kentuckians for the Commonwealth was active in Lexington when I arrived, though they were smaller than they are now. They represented a more moderate, liberal electoral approach compared to the radical punk circles I was involved with. I saw them as part of the broader political ecosystem, but their strategies were quite different from the direct action and Marxist organizing I pursued. They were one of the few established groups in town during my early years here.

In 2014, I helped found the Kentucky Workers League and served as the first chair of its steering committee. It was an explicitly Marxist organization focused on strategic political organizing in Kentucky. Unfortunately, the group was not viable and the experience left me disillusioned with radical organizing in Lexington. That failure pushed me to start my own LLC to pursue activism through a different, more pragmatic approach.
